short_description: Conduct initial air measurements from mobile platforms to look at pollution in the areas of traffic concern for a review with traffic information
action_description: Conduct initial air measurements from mobile platforms to look at pollution in the areas of traffic concern for a review with traffic information
notes:
Comprehensive air monitoring activities (including mobile and fixed-site monitoring) have been conducted in this community by South Coast AQMD and its contractor. Since the initiation of Community Air Monitoring Plan (CAMP) implementation in Quarter 3 2019, comprehensive mobile monitoring surveys have continued to be conducted in the community measuring diesel exhaust emission markers such as particulate matter (PM), black carbon (BC), ultrafine particles (UFP), and nitrogen dioxide (NO2), including contractor's (Aclima) intensive (24/7) measurements from July 1, 2021 through September 30, 2021, covering the entire community. Additional mobile monitoring surveys have been conducted with South Coast AQMD's multi-metal mobile platform (MMMP) that is also equipped with instrumentation to measure diesel exhaust emission markers from June 2022 through September 2023. An update to the MMMP dashboard was made in Q1 2025 to include pollutants related to diesel exhaust emission markers. Additionally, a fixed-site monitoring station was established at Resurrection Church to track concentration trends. Continuous measurements were performed at this site from Quarter 3 2019 through Quarter 4 2023.

Updates have been provided at August 2019, October 2019, August 2020 Quarter 3, August 2021 Quarter 3, November 2021 Quarter 4, February 2022 Quarter 1, and November 2022 Quarter 4 CSC Meetings. An update regarding the termination of measurements at Resurrection Church was provided to the CSC in the 2023 Quarter 4 CSC meeting. A summary of the monitoring efforts and key findings from this objective was presented at the May 2024 Quarter 2 CSC Meeting.

notes:
June 2019 - Initiated potential collaboration with City of Los Angeles 

May 2020 - Continued discussions with City of Los Angeles regarding community plan update 

In South Coast AQMD's role as commenting agency pursuant to California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA), South Coast AQMD reviews CEQA documents prepared by city/county planning departments and provides comments and guidance on how to minimize truck emissions and truck traffic. Projects undergoing CEQA review are identified by land use and AB 617 community, and are tracked in a monthly report to the South Coast AQMD Governing Board, Intergovernmental Review (IGR) of Environmental Documents, and CEQA Lead Agency Projects. 

South Coast AQMD developed a Frequently Asked Questions/Guidance (FAQ) webpage that is available here: https://www.aqmd.gov/home/rules-compliance/ceqa/frequently-asked-questions, and a Quick Reference Guide for Land Use Projects and Activities in AB 617 Communities, which is available here: https://www.aqmd.gov/docs/default-source/ceqa/documents/ab617-quickreferenceguide-feb2025.pdf). Both the FAQ and the Quick Reference Guide have been shared with the CSC and relevant land use agencies.

Also, South Coast AQMD developed an instructional video on truck routes to solicit feedback from the CSC to identify neighborhood streets and corridors which may benefit from restricted truck access. Other informational material such as truck traffic maps were developed and provided to community members as well to assist with the decision-making process. In addition to sharing ALPR data with transportation agencies, South Coast AQMD will compile and share feedback received from the CSC regarding suggested locations for truck access restrictions and report on developments in future reporting periods.

action_description: Reduce Leaks from Oil Tankers: Use optical gas imaging technology, air measurements, and other information to identify leaks from oil tankers to enforce Rule 1142; evaluate amending Rule 1142 to minimize fugitive emission leaks
notes:
Discussion between the US Coast Guard and South Coast AQMD about Rule 1142 - Marine Tank Vessel Operations and South Coast AQMD's jurisdiction, occurred in July 2020.

South Coast AQMD conducts regular surveillance of maritime activities and vessel boarding for on-site inspections.

Rule 1142 already requires vessels to identify, mitigate and prevent fugitive vapor leaks from marine tank vessel- no rule amendment needed.

South Coast AQMD updated the CSC during the February 2019, December 2020 and June 2024 CSC Meetings.

An update on the completed objective was provided to the CSC at the June 2024 Quarter 2 CSC Meeting. 

Only Noted: Rule 1142 - Marine Tank Vessel Operations was assessed and an amendment is not needed at this time

action_description: Conduct mobile air measurements around oil drilling and production wells, prioritizing the locations identified by the CSC, post data on a dedicated webpage on the South Coast AQMD website within 30 days, share data with partner agencies to help inform efforts, and update the CSC
notes:
Based on CSC input, South Coast AQMD defined three main regions within the community for mobile monitoring. Mobile monitoring for volatile organic compounds (VOC) and benzene, toluene, ethylbenzene, and xylenes (BTEX) near and around oil wells in these regions begun in September 2019 and is ongoing on a quarterly basis (mobile monitoring surveys were paused from approximately mid-March 2020 through early March 2021 due to restrictions due to COVID-19 pandemic). If a leak is detected, South Coast AQMD reports internally to mitigate leaks and confirm elevated levels are reduced. 
 
 Updates on mobile survey results and air monitoring data in a form of concentration maps have been provided at the October 2019 Quarter 4, May 2020 Quarter 2, May 2021 Quarter 2, August 2021 Quarter 3, November 2021 Quarter 4, February 2022 Quarter 1, May 2022 Quarter 2, August 2022 Quarter 3, November 2022 Quarter 3, February 2023 Quarter 1, May 2023 Quarter 2, August 2023 Quarter 3, November 2023 Quarter 4, February 2024 Quarter 1, October 2024 Quarter 4, and and February 2025 Quarter 1 CSC Meetings.

short_description: The City of Oakland conducts a study regarding development fees for environmental mitigations.

The City of Oakland, in partnership with the Steering Committee, CARB and the Air District, studies the exposure reduction benefit of requiring solid or vegetative barriers to be incorporated into site design between buildings and sources of air pollution (for example, a freeway).

The Air District researches actions that are potentially exposure-reducing, such as: 1) an engineering evaluation of exhaust stacks and/or vents to determine if relocation will reduce local exposure; (2) a study to determine if smart air filtration systems can reduce exposure by in-taking air during daily non-peak vehicle travel times, such as between midnight and four a.m.; and (3) a study of the potential air quality benefits of a centralized package delivery site such as personal lockers by 2025.

The Air District will continue to engage in environmental review processes for development projects in West Oakland, such as the Oakland A’s Ballpark and the MacArthur Maze Vertical Clearance Project, including coordinating with community partners and lead agency staff, providing data and technical assistance, and reviewing and commenting on CEQA documents through 2025.

short_description: The Port of Oakland, as part of the 2020 and Beyond Seaport Air Quality Plan, supports the transition to zero-emission drayage truck operations, including setting interim year targets out to 2035, coordinating an extensive zero-emission truck commercialization effort, working with the City of Oakland to amend local ordinances to increase the allowable weight limits for single-axle, zero-emission trucks on local streets located within the Port and the Oakland Army Base/Gateway areas, and developing an investment plan for needed upgrades to the Port’s electrical infrastructure.
action_description: The Port of Oakland, as part of the 2020 and Beyond Seaport Air Quality Plan, supports the transition to zero-emission drayage truck operations, including setting interim year targets out to 2035, coordinating an extensive zero-emission truck commercialization effort, working with the City of Oakland to amend local ordinances to increase the allowable weight limits for single-axle, zero-emission trucks on local streets located within the Port and the Oakland Army Base/Gateway areas, and developing an investment plan for needed upgrades to the Port’s electrical infrastructure. The Port of Oakland also works with the California Public Utilities Commission and the California Energy Commission to study the development of time-of-day electric rate structures favorable to truck operators.
notes:
1. Major Investment in Clean Energy Infrastructure at the Port
The California Transportation Commission approved $42 million for the Green Micro-Grid Project, which will dramatically expand the Port’s ability to support electric vehicles—from 50 to around 1,000—modernize grid connections, and deliver community benefits like backup power and improved air quality.

2. $119 Million Awarded for Terminal Modernization and Zero-Emission Equipment
Through the Port Freight Infrastructure Program (PFIP), the Port received $119 million to upgrade maritime facilities, electrify cargo handling equipment, and modernize roadways—across four major improvement segments including wharf infrastructure and outer harbor terminals.

3. Ongoing Community Partnership to Guide Zero-Emissions Transition
In May 2023, the Port and the West Oakland Sustainable Port Collaborative formalized a partnership to coordinate planning and grant efforts for electrification and community environmental benefits. They now meet monthly as the Port Community Electrification and Zero Emissions Committee.

short_description: The City of Oakland, consistent with the West Oakland Truck Management Plan: 1) improves training for police officers, community resource officers, and parking control technicians who issue truck and trailer parking tickets; 2) changes the parking regulations so they are easier to enforce; 3) increases truck parking fines; 4) targets enforcement at specific times and locations; and 5) improves signage directing drivers to available truck parking.
action_description: The City of Oakland, consistent with the West Oakland Truck Management Plan: 1) improves training for police officers, community resource officers, and parking control technicians who issue truck and trailer parking tickets; 2) changes the parking regulations so they are easier to enforce; 3) increases truck parking fines; 4) targets enforcement at specific times and locations; and 5) improves signage directing drivers to available truck parking.
notes:
At its April 19, 2022, hearing Oakland City Council adopted new truck parking regulations for West Oakland consistent with the TMP. On August 27, 2022, the City hosted a community meeting to roll out the truck parking regulations including reviewing enforcement protocols. As of Sept. 2022, new truck parking signage has been installed and parking technicians have been trained on the new truck parking regulations. On July 5, 2022, City Council accepted the Sustainable Transportation Equity Project (STEP) grant from the California Air Resources Board. This grant funds work to implement components of the Truck Management Plan, including truck route signage plan and installation, engagement and outreach, and truck and trailer parking enforcement training. OakDOT will use the grant funds to purchase a vehicle license reader (in fall 2022 or winter 2022) to support enforcement efforts.

No update (Q1 2024)

notes:
SDAPCD

Establish Smart Leak Detection and Repair (LDAR) techniques, such as Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), Ultraviolet Differential Optical Absorption Spectroscopy (UVDOAS), Solar Occultation Flux (SOF) and infrared cameras, to identify, quantify, and locate leaks in real-time allow for faster repair of equipment. Action 2: Conduct Refinery Air Measurements to Identify and Address VOC Leaks. (2/2) Utilize more efficient and effective leak detection systems known as advanced measurement techniques (Smart LDAR), such as Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), Ultraviolet Differential Optical Absorption Spectroscopy (UVDOAS), Solar Occultation Flux (SOF) and infrared cameras, to identify, quantify, and locate VOC leaks in real time, allowing for faster repair in a manner that is less time consuming, labor intensive, and potentially more comprehensive than traditional LDAR
action_description: Establish Smart Leak Detection and Repair (LDAR) techniques, such as Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), Ultraviolet Differential Optical Absorption Spectroscopy (UVDOAS), Solar Occultation Flux (SOF) and infrared cameras, to identify, quantify, and locate leaks in real-time allow for faster repair of equipment
notes:
As part of implementation for South Coast AQMD Rule 1180 - Refinery Fenceline and Community Air Monitoring, South Coast AQMD worked with all refineries in this community to develop and implement fenceline air monitoring systems based on Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and differential optical absorption spectroscopy (DOAS) technologies. Further, solar occultation flux technology (SOF) was utilized in 2021-2022 to a establish volatile organic compound (VOC) emission baseline and will be utilized in future years to track emission reduction goals.
 
 Fenceline systems were designed in a way to detect, characterize and measure fenceline concentrations of fugitive emission plumes crossing refinery fencelines, with data provided to the public in near real-time. These systems became operational in January 2020, with no planned sunset of operations. 
 
 Updates have been provided at January 2020 Quarter 1, August 2020 Quarter 3, August 2021 Quarter 3, and February 2022 Quarter 1 CSC Meetings. A summary of the monitoring efforts and key findings from this objective was presented at the June 2024 Quarter 2 CSC Meeting.
